name: migrate
context: Generate, apply, and manage Prisma database migrations from schema changes
info: Prisma Migrate translates changes in your `schema.prisma` file into versioned SQL migration files and applies them to your database. In development, `prisma migrate dev` creates migration files, applies them, and regenerates the client. In production, `prisma migrate deploy` applies pending migrations without generation. For rapid prototyping, `prisma db push` pushes schema changes directly without creating migration files. The migration history lives in `prisma/migrations/` with timestamped directories each containing a `migration.sql` file. A shadow database is used in development to validate migrations. MongoDB does not use migrations — use `db push` instead. See [[prisma/schema]] for defining models and [[prisma/troubleshooting]] for migration error codes (P3xxx).
body:
# Prisma Migrate

Prisma Migrate generates versioned SQL migration files from your [[prisma/schema]] changes and applies them to your database.

## Development Workflow

```bash
# 1. Edit prisma/schema.prisma
# 2. Generate and apply migration
npx prisma migrate dev --name add-user-table

# This does three things:
#   a) Generates prisma/migrations/<timestamp>_add_user_table/migration.sql
#   b) Applies the migration to your dev database
#   c) Regenerates Prisma Client
```

## Production Deployment

```bash
# Apply all pending migrations (no generation, no shadow DB)
npx prisma migrate deploy
```

**Critical difference:** `migrate dev` is for development only — it uses a shadow database, can reset data, and generates new migrations. `migrate deploy` is production-safe — it only applies existing migration files sequentially.

## All Commands

| Command | Purpose | Environment |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| `npx prisma migrate dev --name <name>` | Create + apply migration | Development |
| `npx prisma migrate dev --create-only` | Create migration without applying | Development |
| `npx prisma migrate deploy` | Apply pending migrations | Production/CI |
| `npx prisma migrate reset` | Drop DB, reapply all migrations + seed | Development |
| `npx prisma migrate reset --skip-seed` | Reset without seeding | Development |
| `npx prisma migrate status` | Show migration status | Any |
| `npx prisma migrate diff` | Compare two schema states, output SQL | Any |
| `npx prisma migrate resolve --applied <name>` | Mark migration as applied | Recovery |
| `npx prisma migrate resolve --rolled-back <name>` | Mark migration as rolled back | Recovery |
| `npx prisma db push` | Push schema directly (no migration files) | Prototyping |
| `npx prisma db push --accept-data-loss` | Push even with destructive changes | Prototyping |
| `npx prisma db pull` | Introspect DB into schema | Any |
| `npx prisma db execute --file script.sql` | Run raw SQL file | Any |

## Migration Directory Structure

```
prisma/
  schema.prisma
  migrations/
    migration_lock.toml              # Locks provider (e.g., postgresql)
    20240101120000_init/
      migration.sql                  # CREATE TABLE statements
    20240102150000_add_posts/
      migration.sql                  # ALTER TABLE statements
    20240103090000_add_indexes/
      migration.sql                  # CREATE INDEX statements
```

Migration files are pure SQL. You can edit them before applying (when using `--create-only`) to add custom SQL like data transformations, triggers, or views.

## Shadow Database

In development, `migrate dev` creates a temporary shadow database to:
1. Validate that migrations can be applied from scratch
2. Detect schema drift between your migration history and current schema

The shadow database requires `CREATE DATABASE` permissions. It is **not used** by `migrate deploy`.

## Prototyping with db push

```bash
# Push schema changes directly — no migration files created
npx prisma db push

# Accept data loss (e.g., dropping a column)
npx prisma db push --accept-data-loss
```

Use `db push` for rapid iteration when you don't need migration history. Switch to `migrate dev` when you need reproducible, versioned migrations.

## Introspection with db pull

```bash
# Generate schema from existing database
npx prisma db pull
```

Introspection reads your database structure and writes models into `schema.prisma`. Useful for adopting Prisma in an existing project or syncing after manual DB changes.

## Resetting the Database

```bash
# Drop all tables, reapply migrations, run seed
npx prisma migrate reset

# Skip the confirmation prompt
npx prisma migrate reset --force
```

## Recovering from Failed Migrations

If a migration partially applied and left the database in a bad state:

```bash
# Fix the issue manually, then mark as applied
npx prisma migrate resolve --applied 20240101120000_init

# Or roll back and try again
npx prisma migrate resolve --rolled-back 20240101120000_init
```

## MongoDB Note

MongoDB does not support SQL migrations. Use `npx prisma db push` for all schema management with MongoDB.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|--------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | Target database for migrations |

sections.title: Development migration workflow
sections.body: Run `npx prisma migrate dev --name descriptive-name` after every schema change. This generates a timestamped migration directory with a `migration.sql` file, applies it, and regenerates the client. Use `--create-only` to generate without applying — useful when you need to edit the SQL before applying.

sections.title: Production deployment
sections.body: Run `npx prisma migrate deploy` in your CI/CD pipeline or deployment script. This applies all pending migrations in order. It never generates new migrations and doesn't use a shadow database. Ensure your `DATABASE_URL` points to the production database. Migrations should be committed to version control.

sections.title: Prototyping with db push
sections.body: Use `npx prisma db push` for rapid prototyping when migration history isn't important. It pushes schema changes directly to the database without creating migration files. Use `--accept-data-loss` when changes would drop data (like removing a column). Switch to `migrate dev` when you're ready for reproducible deployments.

sections.title: Resetting the database
sections.body: Run `npx prisma migrate reset` to drop all tables, reapply all migrations from scratch, and run the seed script. Use `--skip-seed` to skip seeding. This is destructive and only for development — never run in production. Use `--force` to skip the confirmation prompt.

sections.title: Migration recovery
sections.body: If a migration fails partway through, the database may be in an inconsistent state. Use `prisma migrate resolve --applied <name>` to mark a migration as successfully applied (after manually fixing the DB), or `--rolled-back <name>` to mark it as rolled back. Check status with `prisma migrate status`.

links.context: Edit the schema before running migrations
Seed script runs after migrate reset
P3xxx error codes for migration failures

related.note: Schema changes drive the migration workflow
Seeding runs after migrate reset to repopulate data

scripts.name: Run Prisma Migration
scripts.description: Generate and apply a Prisma migration with a descriptive name

MIGRATION_NAME.description: Descriptive name for the migration (e.g., add-user-table)

createOnly.description: Generate migration without applying (dev mode only)

migrationDir.description: Path to the generated migration directory

depends.reason: Migrations are generated from schema changes
